title: Murder of Ahmed Hussein-Suale
description:
Ahmed Hussein-Suale, an undercover journalist who worked with Anas Aremeyaw Anas on a documentary exposing corruption in Ghanaian football, was shot and killed by unknown gunmen on a motorcycle in Madina, Accra.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ahmed_Hussein-Suale)

title: Murder of Major Mahama
description:
Captain Maxwell Adam Mahama, a Ghanaian military officer, was lynched by a mob in Denkyira-Obuasi who mistook him for an armed robber. Multiple suspects were arrested and tried for his murder.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lynching_of_Maxwell_Mahama)

title: Murder of J.B. Danquah-Adu
description:
Joseph Boakye Danquah-Adu, a member of parliament for Abuakwa North constituency, was stabbed to death at his home in Accra. Daniel Asiedu was arrested and charged with his murder.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/J.B._Danquah-Adu)

title: Takoradi Girls Kidnapping
description:
Between 2018 and 2019, four young women were kidnapped in Takoradi, Western Region. Nigerian suspect Samuel Udoetuk Wills and an accomplice were arrested. The remains of the victims were later recovered.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Takoradi_Girls_kidnapping)

title: Assassination of Oumar Lamine Badji
description:
Oumar Lamine Badji, a forestry official and political figure in Senegal's Casamance region, was killed while traveling in the Casamance area. His death was linked to ongoing separatist conflict in the region.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conflict_in_Casamance)

title: Murder of Ndiaga Diouf
description:
Ndiaga Diouf, a student activist, was killed during clashes at the home of Bartheleemy Dias, a Senegalese opposition politician, in Dakar. Dias was convicted of the murder in a politically charged trial.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barth%C3%A9l%C3%A9my_Dias)

title: Hissene Habre Trial
description:
Former Chadian president Hissene Habre was arrested in Dakar, Senegal, and tried by the Extraordinary African Chambers for crimes against humanity, war crimes, and torture committed during his rule in Chad from 1982 to 1990. He was convicted in 2016.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hiss%C3%A8ne_Habr%C3%A9)

title: Grand Bassam Beach Attack
description:
Al-Qaeda in the Islamic Maghreb militants attacked the beach resort town of Grand-Bassam near Abidjan, killing 19 people including tourists and locals. Several perpetrators were killed and others arrested.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2016_Grand-Bassam_shootings)

title: Assassination of Robert Guei
description:
Robert Guei, former military leader of Ivory Coast, was killed along with members of his family during the outbreak of the First Ivorian Civil War. Government forces were suspected of carrying out the killings in Abidjan.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Gu%C3%A9%C3%AF)

title: Murder of Desire Tagro
description:
Desire Tagro, the Interior Minister under Laurent Gbagbo, was killed after Gbagbo's capture during the 2010-2011 Ivorian crisis. He was reportedly shot while in custody of pro-Ouattara forces in Abidjan.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Ivorian_crisis)

title: Murder of Gregoire Doulougou
description:
Gregoire Doulougou, a French-Canadian journalist, was found murdered in a hotel in Yaounde, Cameroon. His death was linked to an investigation he was conducting into corruption. A suspect was eventually identified.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cameroon)

title: Kidnapping of Amadou Ali's Wife
description:
Boko Haram militants kidnapped the wife of Cameroon's Vice Prime Minister Amadou Ali from the town of Kolofata in the Far North region. She was released after negotiations. The kidnapping was part of a broader campaign by Boko Haram in Cameroon.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boko_Haram_insurgency)

title: Ngarbuh Massacre
description:
At least 21 civilians, including 13 children, were killed in an attack on the village of Ngarbuh in the Northwest Region of Cameroon. A government investigation confirmed that members of the Cameroonian military participated in the massacre alongside armed separatist fighters.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ngarbuh_massacre)

title: Assassination of Laurent-Desire Kabila
description:
President Laurent-Desire Kabila of the Democratic Republic of Congo was shot and killed by one of his bodyguards, Rashidi Kasereka, at the Marble Palace in Kinshasa. Multiple suspects were tried by a military tribunal.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assassination_of_Laurent-D%C3%A9sir%C3%A9_Kabila)

title: Murder of Floribert Chebeya
description:
Floribert Chebeya, a prominent Congolese human rights activist, was found dead in his car in Kinshasa after being summoned to a meeting with the police chief. Several police officers were convicted for his murder.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Floribert_Chebeya)

title: Murder of Luc Nkulula
description:
Luc Nkulula, a journalist working for Radio Liberte in Kinshasa, was murdered after reporting on corruption and political issues in the DR Congo. His killing remains unsolved and is believed to be linked to his journalistic work.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo)

title: Murder of Daudi Ngeleja
description:
Daudi Ngeleja, a local councilor in Dar es Salaam, was killed in a politically motivated attack in Tanzania. His murder was tied to local political rivalries and remains unsolved.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crime_in_Tanzania)

title: Assassination of Amina Chifupa
description:
Amina Chifupa, a Tanzanian businesswoman and socialite, was shot dead by unknown assailants in Dar es Salaam. Her murder sparked public outcry and debate about rising gun violence in Tanzania's largest city.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crime_in_Tanzania)

title: 2013 Arusha Church Bombing
description:
A bomb exploded during a Catholic mass at St. Joseph's Church in Arusha, Tanzania, injuring over 60 people. The attack raised fears about religious extremism in the country, and investigations failed to conclusively identify the perpetrators.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2013_Arusha_church_bombing)

title: 2010 Kampala Bombings
description:
Twin bomb attacks at a rugby club and a restaurant in Kampala during the 2010 FIFA World Cup final killed 74 people. Al-Shabaab claimed responsibility. Multiple perpetrators were arrested and convicted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2010_Kampala_bombings)

title: Murder of Andrew Felix Kaweesi
description:
Assistant Inspector General of Police Andrew Felix Kaweesi was shot dead along with his bodyguard and driver by gunmen on a motorcycle in Kulambiro, near Kampala. Multiple suspects were arrested but the case has not been conclusively resolved.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andrew_Felix_Kaweesi)

title: Murder of Joan Kagezi
description:
Joan Kagezi, a Ugandan state prosecutor who was the lead prosecutor in the 2010 Kampala bombings case, was shot and killed by unknown gunmen in the Kiwatule neighborhood of Kampala while returning home with her children.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joan_Kagezi)

title: Murder of Hachalu Hundessa
description:
Hachalu Hundessa, a prominent Ethiopian singer and activist known for songs about the Oromo people's struggle, was shot and killed in Addis Ababa. His murder triggered widespread protests and ethnic violence across Ethiopia. Suspects were arrested and tried.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hachalu_Hundessa)

title: Assassination of General Seare Mekonnen
description:
Ethiopian Army Chief of Staff General Seare Mekonnen was shot and killed by his bodyguard during an attempted coup in Addis Ababa. The attack was part of a coordinated coup attempt in the Amhara region.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2019_Ethiopian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_attempt)

title: Murder of Simegnew Bekele
description:
Simegnew Bekele, the chief engineer of Ethiopia's Grand Renaissance Dam project, was found dead in his car in Addis Ababa with a gunshot wound. Ethiopian authorities investigated the case as a potential murder or suicide, and it remains unresolved.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simegnew_Bekele)

title: 2011 Marrakech Bombing
description:
A bomb exploded at the Argana cafe in Jemaa el-Fnaa square in Marrakech, killing 17 people including several tourists. Adel al-Othmani was convicted and sentenced to death for carrying out the attack.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2011_Marrakesh_bombing)

title: 2003 Casablanca Bombings
description:
A series of coordinated suicide bombings struck five locations in Casablanca, including a Spanish restaurant and a Jewish community center, killing 45 people including 12 bombers. Multiple suspects linked to an Islamist group were arrested and convicted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2003_Casablanca_bombings)

title: Murders of Louisa Vesterager Jespersen and Maren Ueland
description:
Scandinavian tourists Louisa Vesterager Jespersen from Denmark and Maren Ueland from Norway were murdered while camping near Mount Toubkal in Morocco. Three men affiliated with ISIS were convicted and sentenced to death.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Murders_of_Louisa_Vesterager_Jespersen_and_Maren_Ueland)

title: Assassination of Mohamed Brahmi
description:
Mohamed Brahmi, a Tunisian opposition politician and member of the National Constituent Assembly, was assassinated outside his home in Tunis. The killing, attributed to jihadist extremists, triggered a major political crisis in Tunisia.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mohamed_Brahmi)

title: Assassination of Chokri Belaid
description:
Chokri Belaid, a prominent Tunisian leftist opposition leader and lawyer, was shot dead outside his home in Tunis. His assassination sparked massive protests and a political crisis. Suspects linked to Ansar al-Sharia were arrested.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chokri_Bela%C3%AFd)

title: 2015 Bardo National Museum Attack
description:
Two gunmen attacked the Bardo National Museum in Tunis, killing 22 people, mostly European tourists. The Islamic State claimed responsibility. Multiple suspects were convicted in a Tunisian court.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bardo_National_Museum_attack)

title: Murder of Matoub Lounes
description:
Matoub Lounes, a famous Algerian Kabyle singer and political activist, was shot dead at a roadblock in Tizi Ouzou province. While Islamist militants were initially blamed, the case remains controversial and officially unsolved.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lounes_Matoub)

title: 2007 Algiers Bombings
description:
Twin car bombs targeted the Constitutional Council building and the UN offices in Algiers, killing 41 people. Al-Qaeda in the Islamic Maghreb claimed responsibility for the attacks.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2007_Algiers_bombings)

title: Assassination of Mohamed Boudiaf
description:
Mohamed Boudiaf, president of Algeria's High Council of State, was assassinated by a member of his own bodyguard during a televised speech in Annaba. The gunman, Lembarek Boumaarafi, was convicted and sentenced to death.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mohamed_Boudiaf)

title: Yvonne Fletcher Shooting
description:
WPC Yvonne Fletcher, a British policewoman, was shot and killed by gunfire from the Libyan embassy in London during a protest. The incident led to the severing of diplomatic relations between the UK and Libya. No one was successfully prosecuted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Murder_of_Yvonne_Fletcher)

title: Lockerbie Bombing (Pan Am Flight 103)
description:
Pan Am Flight 103 was destroyed by a bomb over Lockerbie, Scotland, killing all 259 people on board and 11 on the ground. Libyan intelligence agent Abdelbaset al-Megrahi was convicted in 2001. The bombing was planned from Tripoli.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pan_Am_Flight_103)

title: Murder of US Ambassador Chris Stevens
description:
US Ambassador J. Christopher Stevens and three other Americans were killed in an attack on the US diplomatic compound in Benghazi, Libya. Ahmed Abu Khattala was captured and convicted in US federal court.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2012_Benghazi_attack)

title: Assassination of John Garang
description:
John Garang, leader of the Sudan People's Liberation Army and newly appointed First Vice President of Sudan, died in a helicopter crash in southern Sudan just weeks after being sworn in. The crash was officially called an accident, but many suspect foul play.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Garang)

title: Khartoum Massacre
description:
Sudanese security forces, primarily the Rapid Support Forces, attacked a pro-democracy sit-in outside military headquarters in Khartoum, killing over 120 protesters. Bodies were dumped in the Nile. No one has been fully held accountable.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Khartoum_massacre)

title: Assassination Attempt on Hosni Mubarak
description:
Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak survived an assassination attempt in Addis Ababa while visiting for an Organization of African Unity summit. The attack was carried out by Egyptian Islamist militants and was traced back to Sudan, leading to international sanctions against the country.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assassination_attempt_on_Hosni_Mubarak)

title: Murder of Ricardo de Mello
description:
Ricardo de Mello, a journalist and director of the Angolan newspaper Imparcial Fax, was killed by gunmen in Luanda. His murder was believed to be connected to his critical reporting on corruption and the Angolan government.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angola)

title: Death of Jose Eduardo dos Santos (Investigation)
description:
Former Angolan President Jose Eduardo dos Santos died in a hospital in Barcelona, Spain. His daughter Tchize dos Santos filed a criminal complaint alleging her father was poisoned, leading to a judicial investigation in both Spain and Angola.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Eduardo_dos_Santos)

title: Murder of Nito Alves Supporters
description:
After a failed coup attempt by Nito Alves against the MPLA government, a massive purge was carried out in Luanda. Thousands of suspected supporters were killed in extrajudicial executions. The full extent of the killings remains undocumented.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nito_Alves)

title: Murder of Carlos Cardoso
description:
Carlos Cardoso, a prominent Mozambican investigative journalist, was shot dead on a street in Maputo while investigating a major banking fraud scandal involving the Commercial Bank of Mozambique. Six men were convicted of his murder.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carlos_Cardoso_(journalist))

title: Murder of Gilles Cistac
description:
Gilles Cistac, a French-born constitutional law professor at Eduardo Mondlane University in Maputo, was shot dead by gunmen who ambushed his car. His murder was believed to be related to his public advocacy for decentralization in Mozambique.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gilles_Cistac)

title: Assassination of Andre Matsangaissa
description:
Andre Matsangaissa, the founder and first leader of the Mozambican National Resistance (RENAMO), was killed during a RENAMO attack on the city of Gorongosa. His death in combat shaped the course of the Mozambican Civil War.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Matsangaissa)

title: Gukurahundi Massacres
description:
The Zimbabwe National Army's 5th Brigade carried out mass killings of Ndebele civilians in Matabeleland and the Midlands during the Gukurahundi period from 1983 to 1987. An estimated 20,000 people were killed. No one has been held accountable.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gukurahundi)

title: Murder of Itai Dzamara
description:
Itai Dzamara, a Zimbabwean journalist and pro-democracy activist, was abducted by suspected state agents from a barbershop in Glen View, Harare. He has never been found and is presumed dead. The case remains unsolved.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Itai_Dzamara)

title: Murder of Cain Nkala
description:
Cain Nkala, a ZANU-PF politician and war veteran leader, was murdered at his home in Bulawayo. Two men were arrested and convicted of his killing. His death was linked to internal political rivalries.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zimbabwe)

title: Murder of Segametsi Mogomotsi
description:
Segametsi Mogomotsi, a 14-year-old schoolgirl from Mochudi, Botswana, was found murdered with body parts removed, suggesting ritual killing. Despite a lengthy investigation and public outrage, no one was convicted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Segametsi_Mogomotsi)

title: Murder of John Kalafatis
description:
John Kalafatis, a prominent Botswana businessman, was shot and killed outside his home in Gaborone. The case was linked to business disputes and organized crime. Suspects were arrested and prosecuted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Botswana)

title: Botswana Serial Killer Case
description:
A series of murders of women in the Gaborone area led to one of Botswana's most extensive criminal investigations. The killings terrorized the community and eventually led to the arrest and conviction of the perpetrator.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crime_in_Botswana)

title: Colleen Stan Kidnapping
description:
While not in Namibia, the most notable criminal case associated with Namibian jurisdiction involves the SWAPO detention camps. During the liberation struggle, SWAPO detained hundreds of its own members in camps in Angola and Zambia on suspicion of being South African spies. Many were tortured and killed.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SWAPO#Detention_of_SWAPO_members)

title: Murder of Juuso Shimhanda
description:
Juuso Shimhanda, a Namibian businessman, was killed in Windhoek in a case that drew significant media attention. The perpetrator was arrested and convicted after a police investigation.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Namibia)

title: Caprivi Strip Attack
description:
Caprivi Liberation Army rebels launched an armed attack on government buildings in Katima Mulilo in the Caprivi Strip. The attack left several dead and led to a state of emergency. Over 100 suspects were arrested and tried for high treason.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Caprivi_conflict)

title: Assassination of Fernando Villavicencio
description:
Fernando Villavicencio, an Ecuadorian presidential candidate and investigative journalist, was shot and killed by gunmen after a campaign rally in Quito. Several suspects linked to organized crime were arrested. A Colombian gang was implicated.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assassination_of_Fernando_Villavicencio)

title: Murder of Efrain Ruales
description:
Efrain Ruales, a popular Ecuadorian television presenter and social media personality, was shot and killed while driving in Guayaquil. His murder shocked the nation and remains unsolved despite investigations linking it to organized crime.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ecuador)

title: TC Television Hostage Crisis
description:
Armed gang members stormed the TC Television studio in Guayaquil during a live broadcast, taking journalists and staff hostage. The incident was broadcast live and led President Noboa to declare an internal armed conflict. The attackers were arrested by security forces.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2024_Ecuadorian_internal_conflict)

title: Murder of Marcelo Quiroga Santa Cruz
description:
Marcelo Quiroga Santa Cruz, a Bolivian writer, politician, and socialist leader, was killed during the coup led by General Luis Garcia Meza. He was captured and executed during the storming of the Bolivian Workers' Central headquarters in La Paz.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marcelo_Quiroga_Santa_Cruz)

title: San Miguel de Huanuni Mine Clashes
description:
Violent clashes between cooperative miners and state miners at the Huanuni tin mine in Bolivia left 16 people dead and over 60 injured. The fighting was over control of mining veins. No individuals were successfully prosecuted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2006_Huanuni_mine_clash)

title: Hotel Las Americas Arms Cache
description:
Bolivian police raided the Hotel Las Americas in Santa Cruz, killing three men alleged to be foreign mercenaries plotting to assassinate President Evo Morales and foment separatism. Eduardo Rozsa Flores, a Bolivian-Hungarian, was among those killed.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2009_Bolivian_plot)

title: Murder of Cecilia Cubas
description:
Cecilia Cubas, daughter of former Paraguayan President Raul Cubas Grau, was kidnapped in Asuncion in 2004 and found dead in 2005. Members of the Patria Libre party were convicted of her kidnapping and murder.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kidnapping_and_murder_of_Cecilia_Cubas)

title: 2012 Curuguaty Massacre
description:
A confrontation between police and landless farmers during an eviction operation in Curuguaty left 11 farmers and 6 police officers dead. The incident led to the impeachment of President Fernando Lugo. Several farmers were convicted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curuguaty_massacre)

title: Murder of Pablo Medina
description:
Pablo Medina, a Paraguayan journalist, and his assistant Antonia Almada were murdered in the department of Canindeyú. The killing was linked to his reporting on drug trafficking and corruption. The shooter was convicted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paraguay)

title: Murder of Eugenio Berrios
description:
Eugenio Berrios, a Chilean biochemist linked to the DINA secret police and the production of chemical weapons, was found dead in Uruguay after being smuggled out of Chile. Chilean and Uruguayan military officers were convicted of his murder.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eugenio_Berr%C3%ADos)

title: Murder of Zelmar Michelini and Hector Gutierrez Ruiz
description:
Uruguayan politicians Zelmar Michelini and Hector Gutierrez Ruiz were kidnapped and murdered in Buenos Aires, Argentina, as part of Operation Condor, the coordinated campaign of political repression by South American dictatorships.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zelmar_Michelini)

title: Kidnapping at the Argentine-Uruguayan Embassy
description:
The Tupamaros, an Uruguayan urban guerrilla group, carried out numerous high-profile kidnappings in Montevideo during the early 1970s, including the kidnapping of British Ambassador Geoffrey Jackson, who was held for eight months before being released.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tupamaros)

title: US Invasion of Panama
description:
The United States invaded Panama to depose military dictator Manuel Noriega, who was wanted on drug trafficking charges. Noriega was captured and convicted in US courts on multiple counts of drug trafficking, racketeering, and money laundering.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_invasion_of_Panama)

title: Murder of Hugo Spadafora
description:
Hugo Spadafora, a Panamanian physician, politician, and opponent of Manuel Noriega, was found decapitated near the Costa Rican border. His murder was attributed to Noriega's forces but no one was formally convicted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Spadafora)

title: Murder of Hector Gallego
description:
Hector Gallego, a Colombian-born Catholic priest working in rural Panama, was abducted from his home in Santa Fe by Panamanian National Guard soldiers and disappeared. His body was never found. The case is believed to be linked to the Torrijos military government.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/H%C3%A9ctor_Gallego)

title: Murder of Jaime Enrique Vallejo Samudio
description:
Costa Rican journalist Parmenio Medina was shot and killed outside his home in San Jose. He hosted a radio program critical of a Catholic radio station's finances. His murder highlighted the dangers facing journalists in the country.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parmenio_Medina)

title: El Psicopata Serial Murders
description:
Jose Manuel Cascante, known as 'El Psicopata,' was convicted of multiple murders in the San Jose area during the mid-1980s. He was one of Costa Rica's most notorious serial killers and his case drew significant national attention.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Costa_Rica)

title: Murder of Maria del Carmen Marin
description:
The murder of a young woman in San Jose highlighted the growing problem of femicide in Costa Rica and led to increased advocacy for women's rights legislation. The case resulted in a conviction and a strengthened legal framework against gender-based violence.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Costa_Rica)

title: Assassination of Bishop Juan Gerardi
description:
Bishop Juan Jose Gerardi Conedera was bludgeoned to death in his garage in Guatemala City, two days after publishing a report on human rights abuses during Guatemala's civil war. Three military officers and a priest were convicted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Juan_Jos%C3%A9_Gerardi_Conedera)

title: Murder of Rodrigo Rosenberg
description:
Rodrigo Rosenberg, a Guatemalan lawyer, was shot and killed while cycling in Guatemala City. Before his death, he recorded a video accusing President Alvaro Colom of ordering his murder. A UN investigation revealed Rosenberg had orchestrated his own assassination.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rodrigo_Rosenberg_Marzano)

title: Dos Erres Massacre
description:
Guatemalan army special forces killed more than 200 men, women, and children in the village of Dos Erres during the civil war. Several former soldiers were eventually convicted decades later in Guatemalan and US courts.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dos_Erres_massacre)

title: Murder of Berta Caceres
description:
Berta Caceres, a Honduran indigenous environmental activist and Goldman Environmental Prize winner, was shot dead in her home in La Esperanza. Several men, including a former military intelligence officer and employees of a dam company, were convicted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berta_C%C3%A1ceres)

title: Murder of Carlos Escaleras
description:
Carlos Escaleras, a Honduran environmental activist and peasant leader, was shot and killed in Tocoa, Colon. His murder was linked to his opposition to palm oil companies' expansion into the Aguan Valley. No one has been convicted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carlos_Escaleras)

title: Assassination of Alfredo Landaverde
description:
Alfredo Landaverde, a former Honduran security adviser who publicly accused police officials of links to drug trafficking, was shot and killed by gunmen on a motorcycle in Tegucigalpa. His murder remains unsolved.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honduras)

title: El Mozote Massacre
description:
The Salvadoran Army's US-trained Atlacatl Battalion killed approximately 1,000 civilians in the village of El Mozote and surrounding areas during the Salvadoran Civil War. A Salvadoran court reopened the case in 2016 and began trials of surviving military officials.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/El_Mozote_massacre)

title: Assassination of Archbishop Oscar Romero
description:
Archbishop Oscar Romero was shot and killed while celebrating mass in a hospital chapel in San Salvador. He was targeted for his vocal criticism of military violence. Former Major Roberto d'Aubuisson was identified as the mastermind. Romero was canonized as a saint in 2018.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%93scar_Romero)

title: Murder of the Jesuit Priests
description:
Six Jesuit priests, their housekeeper, and her daughter were murdered by members of the Atlacatl Battalion at the Central American University in San Salvador during the civil war. Colonel Guillermo Alfredo Benavides was convicted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1989_murders_of_Jesuits_in_El_Salvador)

title: Murder of Pedro Joaquin Chamorro
description:
Pedro Joaquin Chamorro Cardenal, the editor of the opposition newspaper La Prensa and a leading critic of the Somoza dictatorship, was shot and killed in Managua. His assassination helped ignite the Sandinista revolution. Individuals linked to the Somoza regime were convicted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pedro_Joaqu%C3%ADn_Chamorro_Cardenal)

title: Murder of Ben Linder
description:
Benjamin Ernest Linder, an American engineer working on a small hydroelectric project in northern Nicaragua, was killed by Contra rebels. He was the first US citizen killed in the Contra War. No one was prosecuted for his death.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ben_Linder)

title: 2018 Nicaraguan Protest Killings
description:
During widespread anti-government protests in Nicaragua, security forces and pro-government paramilitaries killed over 300 people. The crackdown drew international condemnation from the UN and OAS. No officials have been held accountable.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2018_Nicaraguan_protests)

title: Assassination of Jovenel Moise
description:
President Jovenel Moise of Haiti was assassinated by a group of armed men who stormed his private residence in Port-au-Prince. Multiple suspects, including Colombian mercenaries and Haitian-Americans, were arrested and charged.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assassination_of_Jovenel_Mo%C3%AFse)

title: Assassination of Jean Dominique
description:
Jean Dominique, Haiti's most prominent journalist and owner of Radio Haiti-Inter, was shot and killed in the courtyard of his radio station in Port-au-Prince. Despite investigations, his murder case has been plagued by judicial dysfunction and remains unsolved.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean_Dominique)

title: La Saline Massacre
description:
Armed men carried out a massacre in the La Saline neighborhood of Port-au-Prince, killing at least 71 people. The attack was allegedly linked to government-affiliated gang leaders seeking to suppress anti-corruption protests. No one has been held accountable.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/La_Saline_massacre)

title: Depayin Massacre
description:
A convoy carrying Aung San Suu Kyi and NLD supporters was ambushed near the town of Depayin by a government-organized mob. An estimated 70 to 100 people were killed. Suu Kyi survived but was placed under house arrest. No one was held accountable.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Depayin_massacre)

title: Murder of Ko Ni
description:
Ko Ni, a prominent Muslim lawyer and legal adviser to Aung San Suu Kyi's party, was shot dead at Yangon International Airport. Former military officer Aung Win Zaw and three others were sentenced to death for the assassination.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ko_Ni)

title: 2021 Myanmar Coup Protest Killings
description:
Following the military coup on February 1, 2021, Myanmar security forces killed over 1,500 protesters and bystanders in a violent crackdown across the country. The violence was particularly severe in Yangon, Mandalay, and other major cities.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2021_Myanmar_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat)

title: Khmer Rouge Genocide (Tuol Sleng)
description:
The Khmer Rouge regime operated the S-21 Tuol Sleng prison in Phnom Penh where an estimated 17,000 people were tortured and executed. Kaing Guek Eav (Duch), the commander of S-21, was convicted by the Extraordinary Chambers in the Courts of Cambodia in 2010.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tuol_Sleng)

title: Murder of Chea Vichea
description:
Chea Vichea, president of the Free Trade Union of Workers of the Kingdom of Cambodia, was shot dead while reading a newspaper at a newsstand in Phnom Penh. Two men were convicted but later acquitted on appeal due to lack of evidence. The real killers remain unidentified.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chea_Vichea)

title: Murder of Kem Ley
description:
Kem Ley, a prominent Cambodian political commentator and activist, was shot dead at a gas station in Phnom Penh. Oeuth Ang, a former military officer, was convicted of the murder, but many believe the killing was politically motivated and the real masterminds were not brought to justice.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kem_Ley)

title: Disappearance of Sombath Somphone
description:
Sombath Somphone, a prominent Laotian civil society leader and recipient of the Ramon Magsaysay Award, was stopped at a police checkpoint in Vientiane and abducted. CCTV footage showed him being forced into another vehicle. His fate remains unknown.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sombath_Somphone)

title: Hmong Insurgency Killings
description:
An ambush on a bus in Vang Vieng killed several passengers including two foreign journalists. The attack was linked to the ongoing Hmong insurgency against the Lao government. The journalists were covering the conflict when they were killed.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hmong_insurgency_in_Laos)

title: Arrest of Bounthanh Thammavong
description:
Bounthanh Thammavong, a former Laotian provincial governor, was arrested and charged with corruption and embezzlement of state funds. His case was one of the most high-profile anti-corruption actions in Laos and resulted in a conviction.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laos)

title: Mongolian Death Penalty Cases
description:
Following disputed parliamentary elections in 2008, riots erupted in Ulaanbaatar resulting in five deaths. The government declared a state of emergency. Several rioters and police officers faced criminal charges in the aftermath of the violence.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008_Mongolian_legislative_election_protests)

title: Murder of Zorig Sanjaasuren
description:
Zorig Sanjaasuren, a prominent Mongolian politician known as the 'father of Mongolian democracy,' was stabbed to death in his apartment in Ulaanbaatar. Despite being one of Mongolia's most high-profile murder cases, it has never been conclusively solved.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zorig)

title: Enkhbayar Corruption Case
description:
Former Mongolian President Nambaryn Enkhbayar was arrested and charged with corruption, including misappropriation of state property and abuse of power. He was convicted and sentenced to prison in a case seen as a landmark anti-corruption effort.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nambaryn_Enkhbayar)
